data_quality_notes: SUBSTITUTED PROXY: The hypothesis specifies 'proportion of MRFs with AI/robotic sorting' which has no systematic global database. Used World Bank NV.MNF.TECH.ZS.UN - Medium/High-tech manufacturing (% manufacturing value added) as a structural proxy for industrial technology and automation capacity. This is a distant proxy — medium/high-tech manufacturing share reflects a country's industrial sophistication which correlates with capacity to adopt advanced recycling technology. Partial correlation controlling for log(GDP/capita) could not be computed (numerical error). Reference URL checks: URL accessible (HTTP 200): https://resource-recycling.com/analysis/2026/02/12/the-cyber-physical-mrf-ai-and-robotics-reshape-e-waste-recovery/;   - Relevant MRF/recycling/robotics content confirmed; URL accessible (HTTP 200): https://ecostar.eu.com/municipal-solid-waste-treatment-around-the-world-ecostar/;   - Relevant MRF/recycling/robotics content confirmed. The Resource Recycling article describes facility-level improvements (85%→90% recovery) based on Ecostar HDDS technology across 130+ facilities — not a country-level dataset. The bivariate correlation likely reflects GDP-driven industrialization rather than MRF automation specifically.
summary: MRF automation adoption (proxied by World Bank NV.MNF.TECH.ZS.UN - Medium/High-tech manufacturing (% manufacturing value added)) tested against WRR. Pearson r=0.645 (p=0.0000), Spearman rho=0.673 (p=0.0000), n=397 obs / 113 countries. Partial correlation unavailable. The specific proxy (% MRFs with AI/robotics) has no global dataset; substitution introduces significant measurement error. Literature evidence from Resource Recycling (2026) is industry-specific, not peer-reviewed, covering 130+ Ecostar facilities — facility-level, not country-level.
